title: FKBP5 is associated with amygdala volume in the human brain and mood state: A voxel-based morphometry (VBM) study
citation: Hirakawa, H., Akiyoshi, J., Muronaga, M., Tanaka, Y., Ishitobi, Y., Inoue, A., Oshita, H., Aizawa, S., Masuda, K., Higuma, H., Kanehisa, M., Ninomiya, T., & Kawano, Y. (2016). FKBP5 is associated with amygdala volume in the human brain and mood state: A voxel-based morphometry (VBM) study. International Journal of Psychiatry in Clinical Practice, 20(2), 106–115. https://doi.org/10.3109/13651501.2016.1144772

website_card.short_description: A cross-sectional voxel-based morphometry study examining associations between FKBP5 genotypes, amygdala volume, and mood states in healthy volunteers.
dataset_features_summary: T1-weighted MRI scans from 112 healthy Japanese volunteers
Depression severity scores via Beck Depression Inventory (BDI)
Anxiety severity scores via State-Trait Anxiety Inventory (STAI-S and STAI-T)
Mood state assessments using Profile of Mood States (POMS)
Medication status explicitly recorded as none for all participants
website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study analyzed T1-weighted MRI scans from 112 healthy volunteers to investigate how FKBP5 gene variants relate to regional brain volumes, particularly in the amygdala and orbitofrontal cortex. Participants completed standardized psychological assessments for depression, anxiety, and mood states, with all subjects confirmed to have no history of mental illness or current medication use. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI data, FKBP5 SNP genotype information, and corresponding psychological scale scores from a healthy adult cohort.

extraction.notes: Extracted features based on explicit statements in the Methods and Results sections. Age ranges were not explicitly provided in the text, so age_group tags are omitted. Trauma/stress was explicitly noted as not evaluated in this cohort. Clinical outcomes marked unclear as only psychological scale scores were reported without clinical trial endpoints.

title: General and specific effects of early-life psychosocial adversities on adolescent grey matter volume
citation: Walsh, N. D., Dalgleish, T., Lombardo, M. v., Dunn, V. J., van Harmelen, A. L., Ban, M., & Goodyer, I. M. (2014). General and specific effects of early-life psychosocial adversities on adolescent grey matter volume. NeuroImage: Clinical, 4, 308–318. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.nicl.2014.01.001

website_card.short_description: A cross-sectional neuroimaging study examining the association between childhood adversities, recent negative life events, and grey matter volume in healthy adolescents.
dataset_features_summary: T1-weighted MRI scans
Voxel-based morphometry (VBM) analysis
5-HTTLPR genotype data
Childhood adversity and recent negative life event assessments
Depression severity scores (MFQ)
IQ measurements
website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study analyzed T1-weighted MRI scans from 58 healthy adolescents recruited from a population-based cohort to examine how childhood adversities and recent negative life events relate to regional grey matter volume. The researchers found that moderate chronic family-focused adversities were associated with reduced cerebellar grey matter, while recent stress at age 14 correlated with increased prefrontal and cerebellar volumes. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI data, retrospective childhood adversity and life event histories, 5-HTTLPR genotype information, and standardized depression and IQ scores for the adolescent sample.

extraction.notes: Extracted features based on explicit mentions in the abstract, methods, and results. Participants were healthy adolescents; T1-weighted MRI and VBM were used; genetic (5-HTTLPR), depression (MFQ), IQ, and trauma/life stress data were collected. Study design was cross-sectional.

extraction_notes: Extracted features based on explicit mentions in the abstract, methods, and results. Participants were healthy adolescents; T1-weighted MRI and VBM were used; genetic (5-HTTLPR), depression (MFQ), IQ, and trauma/life stress data were collected. Study design was cross-sectional.

title: Reduced prefrontal cortical gray matter volume in young adults exposed to harsh corporal punishment
citation: Tomoda, A., Suzuki, H., Rabi, K., Sheu, Y.-S., Polcari, A., & Teicher, M. H. (2009b). Reduced prefrontal cortical gray matter volume in young adults exposed to harsh corporal punishment. NeuroImage, 47(Suppl 2), T66–T71. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neuroimage.2009.03.005

website_card.short_description: A cross-sectional voxel-based morphometry study comparing prefrontal gray matter volume in young adults exposed to harsh corporal punishment versus healthy controls.
dataset_features_summary: High-resolution T1-weighted MRI scans
Voxel-based morphometry (VBM) analysis of gray matter volume
Childhood trauma exposure history via Life Experiences Questionnaire
Cognitive assessment data (WAIS-III Performance IQ)
Medication status and psychiatric screening (SCID-I/II)
website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study used high-resolution T1-weighted MRI to compare gray matter volume in the prefrontal cortex between 23 young adults exposed to harsh corporal punishment during childhood and 22 healthy controls. The exposed group showed significantly reduced gray matter volume in medial and dorsolateral frontal regions, which correlated with performance IQ scores. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI scans, detailed childhood trauma exposure histories, standardized cognitive test results (specifically WAIS-III), and confirmed unmedicated status for all participants.

citation: Zou, L., Wu, X., Tao, S., Yang, Y., Zhang, Q., Hong, X., Xie, Y., Li, T., Zheng, S., & Tao, F. (2022). website_card.short_description: A cross-sectional study examining how physical activity and brain gray matter volume moderate the relationship between problematic mobile phone use and psychological symptoms in college students.
dataset_features_summary: T1-weighted MRI data from 251 young adult college students
Voxel-based morphometry (VBM) analysis of gray matter volume
Self-reported measures of problematic mobile phone use, physical activity, and psychological symptoms (depression, anxiety, stress)
Demographic covariates including age, gender, residential area, family income, and academic performance
website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study analyzed T1-weighted MRI scans from 251 college students to investigate how physical activity and brain gray matter volume moderate the link between problematic mobile phone use and psychological symptoms. Results indicated that higher physical activity levels were associated with greater gray matter volume in specific regions (e.g., left insula) and reduced the negative impact of problematic phone use on depression, anxiety, and stress scores. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted structural MRI scans, standardized questionnaires for mobile phone use and psychological symptoms, and demographic covariate data.

citation: Ma, L., & Zhang, C. (2022). The function and structure of precuneus is associated with subjective sleep quality in major depression. Frontiers in Psychiatry, 12. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyt.2021.831524

website_card.short_description: A cross-sectional MRI study investigating the association between subjective sleep quality and precuneus function and structure in major depression patients versus healthy controls.
dataset_features_summary: T1-weighted structural MRI data
Resting-state fMRI data (fALFF)
Voxel-based morphometry (GMV) analysis
Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HAMD) scores
Hamilton Anxiety Rating Scale (HAMA) scores
Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI) scores
Medication status for depression patients
website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study compared 114 individuals with major depressive disorder and 74 healthy controls using resting-state fMRI and T1-weighted structural MRI. The researchers found that poorer subjective sleep quality in the depressed group was associated with lower local neuronal activity (fALFF) and reduced gray matter volume in the bilateral precuneus, whereas these associations were absent in healthy controls. Replication would require access to raw resting-state and structural MRI scans, Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index scores, Hamilton depression/anxiety scale ratings, and medication history for the clinical cohort.

extraction.notes: Extracted features based on explicit mentions of MRI modalities, clinical scales (HAMD, HAMA, PSQI), participant groups (MD vs NCs), and cross-sectional design. Genetic, trauma, cognitive, blood biomarker, and longitudinal data were not collected or reported.

website_card.short_description: This cross-sectional study investigates the neuroanatomical basis linking depression and anxiety symptoms to sleep quality in a cohort of healthy college students using voxel-based morphometry.
dataset_features_summary: T1-weighted MRI scans from 370 healthy young adults
Self-Rating Depression Scale (SDS) scores
Self-Rating Anxiety Scale (SAS) scores
Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI) scores
Regional gray matter volume measurements of the insula
website_card.plain_text_summary: The study analyzed T1-weighted MRI data and self-reported depression, anxiety, and sleep quality scores from 370 healthy college students aged 17 to 27. Results showed that depressive and anxious symptoms were positively correlated with poor sleep quality, and the gray matter volume of the right insula partially mediated this relationship. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI scans, SDS and SAS questionnaire data, PSQI scores, and demographic information for young adult participants.

dataset_features_summary: T1-weighted structural MRI scans
Resting-state fMRI data
Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HAMD) scores

website_card.plain_text_summary: This study used T1-weighted MRI and resting-state functional connectivity scans from 103 remitted major depressive disorder patients to compare brain structure and function between those with adolescent-onset versus adult-onset depression. The researchers found that early-onset patients exhibited increased gray matter volume in the hippocampus, prefrontal cortex, and insula, along with altered functional connectivity patterns. Replication would require access to raw structural and resting-state MRI data, HAMD symptom scores, and precise age-of-onset classifications for remitted MDD participants.

title: Neurobiological predictors for clinical trajectories in fully remitted depressed patients
citation: Blank, T. S., Meyer, B. M., Rabl, U., Schögl, P., Wieser, M., & Pezawas, L. (2021). Neurobiological predictors for clinical trajectories in fully remitted depressed patients. Depression and Anxiety, 38(4), 447–455. https://doi.org/10.1002/da.23108

website_card.short_description: A longitudinal neuroimaging study investigating brain structure and function as predictors of clinical trajectories in fully remitted major depressive disorder patients.
dataset_features_summary: Structural T1-weighted MRI scans acquired at baseline
Voxel-based morphometry (VBM) analysis results for gray matter volume
Resting-state functional connectivity (RSFC) data from independent component analysis
Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HAMD) scores at baseline and 2-year follow-up
Medication history and discontinuation status prior to enrollment
Cognitive assessment scores including German vocabulary scale
website_card.plain_text_summary: This prospective longitudinal study followed 39 fully remitted major depressive disorder patients with a mean age of 26 years, collecting baseline structural T1-weighted MRI and resting-state functional connectivity scans. The main results indicated that increased gray matter volume in the posterior cingulate cortex and stronger default mode network connectivity predicted improvement in residual depressive symptoms, while alterations in the reward circuitry (nucleus accumbens and insula) predicted recurrent major depressive episodes over a two-year period. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI and resting-state fMRI data, longitudinal clinical assessments using the Hamilton Depression Rating Scale, and detailed medication history for remitted MDD patients.

extraction.notes: Extracted features based on explicit mentions of T1-weighted MRI, VBM analysis, rMDD patient cohort, HAMD depression scales, longitudinal follow-up design, and medication history. Genetic, anxiety, trauma, and biomarker data were not reported.

website_card.short_description: This imaging genetics study investigates the association between the NCAN rs1064395 risk variant and limbic gray matter volumes in healthy controls and patients with major depressive disorder.
dataset_features_summary: T1-weighted structural MRI data from two independent German cohorts (healthy subjects n=512, MDD patients n=171).
Genotyping for the NCAN rs1064395 SNP.
Clinical assessments including BDI depression scores and STAI-trait anxiety scores.
Verbal intelligence estimates using a multiple-choice vocabulary test.
Medication status recorded (all MDD patients on antidepressants).
website_card.plain_text_summary: The study analyzed T1-weighted MRI scans and NCAN rs1064395 genotype data from two independent German cohorts comprising 512 healthy adults and 171 hospitalized patients with major depressive disorder. Carriers of the NCAN risk allele exhibited significantly reduced gray matter volumes in the amygdala and hippocampus across both groups, indicating a disease-independent genetic effect on limbic brain structure. Replication would require access to raw structural MRI scans, SNP genotype data for rs1064395, and demographic/clinical covariates such as age, sex, and depression severity scores.

website_card.plain_text_summary: The study analyzed T1-weighted MRI scans from 63 patients with major depressive disorder and 46 healthy controls to examine grey matter volume differences related to alexithymia. Higher alexithymia scores were associated with decreased fusiform gyrus volume in MDD patients but increased volume in healthy controls. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI data, TAS-20 alexithymia scores, standardized depression rating scales (HAM-D/BDI), and medication/remission status records for both clinical and control groups.

website_card.plain_text_summary: This study compared treatment-naive adolescent patients with major depressive disorder to healthy controls using multi-voxel proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy to measure brain metabolite ratios in the prefrontal white matter and anterior cingulate gray matter. Depressed adolescents showed significantly lower NAA/Cr and Cho/Cr ratios in the dorsolateral prefrontal white matter, which positively correlated with standardized memory quotient scores. Replication would require access to raw MRS data from adolescent depression patients and matched healthy controls, along with HDRS clinical ratings and memory test scores.

website_card.short_description: A monozygotic twin study using voxel-based morphometry to identify gray matter volume reductions in the amygdala and fusiform gyrus associated with genetic liability for anxiety and depression.
dataset_features_summary: T1-weighted MRI scans from 53 monozygotic twins (concordant, discordant, and healthy control groups)
Voxel-based morphometry analysis of regional gray matter volume
Clinical diagnoses via SCID-I and symptom severity scores using BDI-II and BAI

website_card.plain_text_summary: This study acquired T1-weighted MRI scans from 53 monozygotic twins, including pairs concordant or discordant for lifetime anxiety and depression disorders, alongside healthy control twins. Voxel-based morphometry revealed that gray matter reductions in the bilateral amygdala and fusiform gyrus were specifically linked to genetic liability rather than unique environmental factors. Replication would require access to high-resolution structural MRI data, confirmed monozygotic twin pairs stratified by psychiatric diagnosis, standardized clinical symptom scales (BDI-II, BAI), and DNA samples for zygosity verification.

website_card.short_description: A cross-sectional MRI study examining the association between persistent harsh parenting, subclinical anxiety symptoms, and prefrontal cortex and amygdala anatomy in healthy adolescents.
dataset_features_summary: T1-weighted structural MRI scans acquired at a single timepoint
Longitudinal parent-reported harsh parenting and anxiety symptom trajectories from ages 2.5 to 9 years
Voxel-based morphometry (VBM) and FreeSurfer cortical thickness analyses
Demographic covariates including age, sex, socioeconomic status, IQ, and pubertal stage
website_card.plain_text_summary: This study analyzed T1-weighted MRI scans from 94 healthy adolescents aged 12 to 16 years who were categorized based on persistent levels of harsh parenting and subclinical anxiety symptoms measured longitudinally between ages 2.5 and 9. The researchers found that higher historical harsh parenting was associated with smaller prefrontal cortex and amygdala volumes, while interactions between parenting and anxiety influenced rostral anterior cingulate cortex thickness and amygdala volume. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI data, longitudinal behavioral ratings of parental harshness and child anxiety from early childhood, and demographic/IQ measures for covariate adjustment.

dataset_features_summary: T1-weighted MRI scans acquired on a Siemens 2-T system
Voxel-based morphometry (VBM) analysis for gray and white matter volume quantification
DSM-IV diagnosed late-onset MDD patients versus age-matched healthy controls
Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HDRS) severity scores
Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) cognitive screening scores
Documented history of suicide attempts and illness duration
website_card.plain_text_summary: This study used T1-weighted MRI and voxel-based morphometry to compare brain structure in 70 elderly male patients with late-onset major depression and 26 age-matched healthy controls. The researchers found widespread gray and white matter volume reductions in depressed patients, particularly those with a history of suicide attempts, compared to nondepressed volunteers. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI scans from older adult clinical and control groups, DSM-IV diagnostic criteria documentation, HDRS severity scores, and detailed histories of suicidal behavior.

Healthy control group data
website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study compared T1-weighted MRI scans from 19 elderly participants with subthreshold depression and 17 healthy age-matched controls. VBM analysis revealed significantly reduced gray matter volume in the right parahippocampus of the StD group, which correlated inversely with self-reported depressive symptoms measured by the CES-D scale. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI data, CES-D scores for each participant, and demographic covariates such as age, gender, education, MMSE scores, and total intracranial volume.

website_card.short_description: Cross-sectional study examining the association between subcortical white and gray matter lesion volumes on MRI and functional impairment in older adults with major depression.
dataset_features_summary: Structural MRI scans (T2-weighted dual-echo fast spin echo) for subcortical lesion volumetry
DSM-IV diagnosed unipolar major depression in participants aged 60+
Depression severity scores using MADRS and Hamilton Rating Scale
Cognitive screening via Mini Mental State Examination (MMSE)
Functional impairment measures for basic (BADL) and instrumental activities of daily living (IADL)
website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study analyzed MRI scans from 224 older adults (aged 60+) with unipolar major depression to examine the relationship between subcortical white and gray matter lesion volumes and functional impairment in daily activities. Greater white matter lesion volume was independently associated with impairment in instrumental activities of daily living, even after controlling for age, gender, depression severity, and medical comorbidity. Replication would require access to structural MRI scans (specifically T2-weighted sequences for lesion identification), DSM-IV diagnostic criteria documentation, MADRS or Hamilton depression scores, MMSE cognitive assessments, and standardized BADL/IADL functional status scales.

website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study analyzed T1-weighted MRI scans, plasma eotaxin levels, and cognitive assessments from 27 older adults with major depressive disorder. The main result showed that lower gray matter density in seven specific brain regions fully mediated the association between higher eotaxin levels and poorer set-shifting performance. Replication would require access to raw structural MRI data, peripheral blood samples for chemokine measurement, and standardized executive function test scores from a late-life depression cohort.

Self-reported birth weight measurements
Depression status assessed via PHQ-9 and CIDI-SF criteria

Polygenic risk scores for birth weight
website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study analyzed data from the UK Biobank cohort to examine associations between self-reported birth weight, brain volumes, and depression status in middle-aged adults. Results indicated that higher birth weight was positively associated with larger brain volumes (e.g., gray matter, thalamus) and negatively associated with depression prevalence, with brain volumes mediating this relationship. Replication would require access to UK Biobank phenotypic data for birth weight and depression scales, MRI-derived brain volume metrics, and pre-calculated polygenic risk scores for birth weight.

website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study compared T1-weighted MRI and diffusion tensor imaging scans from 19 elderly euthymic patients with bipolar disorder and 47 healthy controls. The researchers found reduced grey matter concentration in anterior limbic areas and decreased fractional anisotropy in the corpus callosum among patients, alongside standardized neuropsychological assessments showing cognitive deficits. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI and DTI scans, detailed medication histories, illness duration records, and neuropsychological test scores for elderly bipolar patients and age-matched healthy controls.

title: Structural brain differences predict early traumatic memory processing
citation: Gvozdanovic, G., Stämpfli, P., Seifritz, E., & Rasch, B. (2020). Structural brain differences predict early traumatic memory processing. Psychophysiology, 57(1). https://doi.org/10.1111/psyp.13354

website_card.short_description: This study examines how pre-existing structural brain volumes in healthy young women predict early intrusive memories following an experimental trauma film exposure.
dataset_features_summary: High-resolution structural T1-weighted MRI scans acquired prior to trauma exposure
Voxel-based morphometry (VBM) analysis of whole-brain and predefined ROI gray matter volumes
7-day daily intrusion diary tracking frequency, vividness, and distress of intrusive memories

Experimental trauma film paradigm depicting rape and physical assault
website_card.plain_text_summary: The study analyzed structural T1-weighted MRI data from 74 healthy young female participants to examine how pre-existing brain volumes predict early intrusive memories following an experimental trauma film. Results showed that smaller insular volume was associated with more frequent intrusions, while larger lingual gyrus/cerebellum and inferior frontal gyrus/precentral gyrus volumes were positively correlated with intrusion counts. Replication would require access to raw structural MRI scans, standardized depression and anxiety scale scores (BDI, STAI), and a controlled experimental trauma film paradigm with daily intrusion diary tracking.

The neuroanatomy of subthreshold depressive symptoms in Huntington’s disease: A combined diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) and voxel-based morphometry (VBM) study. Psychological Medicine, 44(9), 1867–1878. https://doi.org/10.1017/S003329171300247X

website_card.short_description: A cross-sectional neuroimaging study investigating the brain structure and microstructure underlying subthreshold depressive symptoms in early-stage Huntington's disease.
dataset_features_summary: T1-weighted MRI scans from 61 early-stage HD patients and 40 healthy controls
Diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) data for fractional anisotropy analysis
Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS-SIS) scores for depression and anxiety severity
Cognitive assessment results including TMT, WFL, SDMT, and HVLT
Genetic CAG repeat length data and calculated disease burden metrics
Medication history recording antidepressant and anti-hyperkinetic use
website_card.plain_text_summary: This study analyzed baseline MRI scans from 61 early-stage Huntington's disease patients and 40 healthy controls to identify neuroanatomical correlates of subthreshold depressive symptoms. Participants completed standardized depression and anxiety scales, cognitive assessments, and provided genetic CAG repeat data for disease burden calculation. Replication would require access to T1-weighted MRI and DTI scans, HADS-SIS scores, cognitive test results, and CAG genotype information for both patient and control groups.

citation: Kanayama, N., Asai, T., Nakao, T., Makita, K., Kozuma, R., Uyama, T., Yamane, T., Kadota, H., & Yamawaki, S. (2017). Subjectivity of the anomalous sense of self is represented in gray matter volume in the brain. Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, 11. https://doi.org/10.3389/fnhum.2017.00232

website_card.short_description: This cross-sectional study examines the correlation between gray matter volume and self-related malfunctions measured by the Embodied Sense of Self Scale in healthy adults.
dataset_features_summary: T1-weighted structural MRI scans from 96 healthy participants
Embodied Sense of Self Scale (ESSS) questionnaire scores measuring agency, ownership, and narrative subscales
Voxel-based morphometry (VBM) analysis of gray matter volume
website_card.plain_text_summary: The study analyzed T1-weighted MRI scans and self-reported questionnaire data from 96 healthy young adults to investigate how subjective experiences of self-related malfunctions correlate with brain structure. Voxel-based morphometry revealed that lower gray matter volume in the left posterior insular cortex was significantly associated with higher scores on the ownership subscale of the Embodied Sense of Self Scale. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI data, ESSS questionnaire responses, and demographic information (age, sex) for covariate adjustment.

extraction.notes: Extracted features based on explicit mentions in the text. Healthy sample, single timepoint T1-MRI with VBM analysis, ESSS questionnaire used. No clinical groups, genetics, or longitudinal data reported.

title: Cross-sectional and longitudinal relationships between depressive symptoms and brain atrophy in MS patients
citation: Stuke, H., Hanken, K., Hirsch, J., Klein, J., Wittig, F., Kastrup, A., & Hildebrandt, H. (2016). Cross-sectional and longitudinal relationships between depressive symptoms and brain atrophy in MS patients. Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, 10. https://doi.org/10.3389/fnhum.2016.00622

website_card.short_description: A prospective and cross-sectional study examining the relationship between depressive symptoms and regional brain atrophy in patients with multiple sclerosis using voxel-based morphometry of T1-weighted MRI scans.
dataset_features_summary: High-resolution T1-weighted structural MRI scans acquired at baseline and 17-month follow-up
Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) scores for depressive symptom severity at two timepoints
Expanded Disability Status Scale (EDSS) scores tracking MS disability progression
Medication records including antidepressant use and disease-modifying therapies (e.g., interferon-beta, glatiramer acetate)
Clinical diagnosis of relapsing-remitting or secondary progressive multiple sclerosis
website_card.plain_text_summary: This study analyzed T1-weighted MRI scans from 40 to 44 patients with multiple sclerosis to investigate how depressive symptoms relate to regional brain atrophy over a 17-month period. Depressive symptom severity was measured using the Beck Depression Inventory, and voxel-based morphometry revealed that baseline gray matter volume in prefrontal regions predicted worsening depression, while increasing atrophy in subcortical structures correlated with rising depressive symptoms. Replication would require access to longitudinal T1-weighted MRI data from MS patients, standardized depression scale scores (BDI), disability ratings (EDSS), and medication records.

extraction.notes: Extracted features based on explicit mentions of T1 MRI, VBM analysis, BDI depression scale, EDSS clinical scores, longitudinal follow-up at 17 months, and medication records in Table 1. No healthy controls or genetic/blood biomarker data were reported. MDD was explicitly excluded as the study focused on subclinical depressive symptoms rather than full clinically diagnosed depression.

title: Reduced cerebral blood flow within the default-mode network and within total gray matter in major depression
citation: Orosz, A., Jann, K., Federspiel, A., Horn, H., Höfle, O., Dierks, T., Wiest, R., Strik, W., Müller, T., & Walther, S. (2012). Reduced cerebral blood flow within the default-mode network and within total gray matter in major depression. Brain Connectivity, 2(6), 303–310. https://doi.org/10.1089/brain.2012.0101

website_card.short_description: A study comparing resting-state cerebral blood flow within the default-mode network between major depressive disorder patients and healthy controls using arterial spin labeling MRI.
dataset_features_summary: T1-weighted structural MRI scans
Arterial Spin Labeling (ASL) perfusion MRI data
Beck Depression Inventory (BDI), Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HAMD), and Montgomery-Asberg Depression Rating Scale (MADRS) scores
Medication status and history for MDD patients
Short-term clinical follow-up assessment at 4 weeks
website_card.plain_text_summary: This study used T1-weighted MRI and resting-state arterial spin labeling perfusion scans from 22 major depressive disorder patients and 22 matched healthy controls. The researchers found that absolute cerebral blood flow within the default-mode network was reduced in MDD patients, but this difference disappeared after controlling for whole-brain gray matter perfusion and age; however, depression severity remained inversely correlated with perfusion in specific DMN nodes. Replication would require access to raw ASL perfusion MRI data, T1-weighted structural scans, standardized depression rating scale scores (BDI, HAMD, MADRS), and detailed medication history for the patient group.

extraction.notes: Extracted features based on explicit mentions of T1w MRI acquisition, ASL perfusion imaging, MDD vs healthy control groups, depression rating scales (BDI, HAMD, MADRS), medication status, and short-term clinical follow-up. No genetic, anxiety, trauma, cognitive, or blood biomarker data were reported.

extraction_notes: Extracted features based on explicit mentions of T1w MRI acquisition, ASL perfusion imaging, MDD vs healthy control groups, depression rating scales (BDI, HAMD, MADRS), medication status, and short-term clinical follow-up. citation: Gobbi, C., Rocca, M. A., Riccitelli, G., Pagani, E., Messina, R., Preziosa, P., Colombo, B., Rodegher, M., Falini, A., Comi, G., & Filippi, M. (2014). website_card.short_description: A cross-sectional MRI study examining the relationship between regional gray matter atrophy and depressive symptoms in patients with multiple sclerosis.
dataset_features_summary: 3D T1-weighted structural MRI scans from MS patients and healthy controls
Voxel-based morphometry (VBM) analysis of cortical and subcortical gray/white matter
Standardized depression severity scores (MADRS)
Fatigue severity scores (FSS)
Cognitive assessment data (PASAT)
Disease-modifying medication status recording
website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study analyzed 3D T1-weighted MRI scans from 123 multiple sclerosis patients and 90 healthy controls to determine how regional brain atrophy correlates with depression and fatigue. Depression severity was quantified using the MADRS, while cognitive function was evaluated via the PASAT. Replication would require access to raw structural MRI data, standardized mood and fatigue scale scores, and detailed clinical phenotyping of multiple sclerosis subtypes.

website_card.short_description: A cross-sectional study examining the relationship between perfectionism, gray matter volume in the anterior cingulate cortex, and negative emotion in a nonclinical sample of young adults.
dataset_features_summary: T1-weighted MRI data from 161 healthy university students
Self-reported anxiety (SAS) and depression (SDS) scores
Perfectionism scale scores (CFMPS/FMPS)
Voxel-based morphometry analysis of gray matter volume
website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study analyzed T1-weighted MRI scans from 161 healthy Chinese undergraduate students to investigate the link between perfectionism, brain structure, and negative emotion. The results showed that specific dimensions of perfectionism were positively correlated with self-reported anxiety and depression scores as well as gray matter volume in the anterior cingulate cortex. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI scans, standardized anxiety and depression scale data, and multidimensional perfectionism questionnaire scores from a nonclinical young adult sample.

extraction.notes: Extracted features strictly from explicit mentions in the Methods and Results sections. Population confirmed as healthy/nonclinical young adults (mean age ~20). website_card.short_description: A cross-sectional neuroimaging study investigating the neural substrates linking trait anxiety to delay discounting using VBM and resting-state fMRI in healthy students.
dataset_features_summary: T1-weighted MRI scans
Resting-state functional connectivity data
Trait Anxiety Inventory (TAI) scores
Delay discounting task behavioral data
website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study examined 100 healthy university students using T1-weighted MRI and resting-state fMRI to investigate how trait anxiety relates to delay discounting. Behavioral results showed that higher trait anxiety was positively correlated with steeper delay discounting rates, while VBM analysis revealed positive correlations between trait anxiety and gray matter volumes in the right hippocampus and cerebellum. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI scans, resting-state fMRI data, Trait Anxiety Inventory scores, and individual delay discounting task performance metrics.

citation: Jabbi, M., Arasappan, D., Eickhoff, S. B., Strakowski, S. M., Nemeroff, C. B., & Hofmann, H. A. (2020). Neuro-transcriptomic signatures for mood disorder morbidity and suicide mortality. Journal of Psychiatric Research, 127, 62–74. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpsychires.2020.05.013

website_card.short_description: A neuroimaging-guided postmortem transcriptomic study linking anterior insula gray matter volume loss in mood disorders to gene expression signatures of psychiatric morbidity and suicide mortality.
dataset_features_summary: Coordinate-based VBM meta-analysis data from 47 publications (N=2387 living participants)
Postmortem RNA-sequencing data from 100 brain donors (MDD, BD, controls)
Clinical diagnostic status and Axis-I comorbidity scores
Toxicology/blood alcohol levels
website_card.plain_text_summary: This study combines a voxel-based morphometry meta-analysis of living participants with postmortem RNA-sequencing of 100 brain donors to investigate the molecular basis of mood disorders. The authors identified reduced gray matter volume in the anterior insula cortex across major depressive and bipolar disorder cohorts, which correlated with differential gene expression related to immune, inflammatory, and neurodevelopmental pathways in high morbidity and suicide mortality cases. Replication would require access to coordinate-based VBM meta-analysis data, postmortem brain tissue RNA-sequencing datasets, and detailed clinical diagnostic records including comorbidity and suicide status.

extraction.notes: Extracted features based on explicit mentions of VBM meta-analysis, postmortem RNA-seq, clinical diagnoses, and toxicology. Medication status and trauma/stress were unclear due to lack of explicit reporting or control in the analysis.

citation: Agüera‐Ortiz, L., Hernandez‐Tamames, J. A., Martinez‐Martin, P., Cruz‐Orduña, I., Pajares, G., López‐Alvarez, J., Osorio, R. S., Sanz, M., & Olazarán, J. (2017). website_card.short_description: A cross-sectional MRI study examining the structural correlates of apathy dimensions in patients with advanced Alzheimer's disease.
dataset_features_summary: 3T T1-weighted MRI for voxel-based morphometry
FLAIR and DTI imaging for white matter integrity and vascular damage assessment
Apathy In Dementia-Nursing Home Version Scale (APADEM-NH) total and subscore data
Neuropsychiatric Inventory depression item scores
Severe mini-mental state examination (sMMSE), Global Deterioration Scale, and FAST staging
website_card.plain_text_summary: This study analyzed baseline MRI scans from 37 nursing home patients with moderate to advanced Alzheimer's disease to identify brain structural correlates of apathy. The researchers found that bilateral damage to the corpus callosum and internal capsule, along with right periventricular ischemic lesions, were significantly associated with different dimensions of apathy. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI scans, FLAIR and DTI data, standardized apathy and depression scale scores, and detailed clinical staging information for patients with advanced dementia.

extraction.notes: Features extracted based on explicit mentions in the Methods and Results sections. Medication status marked as unclear because while current medications were part of the center's protocol, they were not explicitly reported or analyzed for this specific cross-sectional analysis.

website_card.short_description: A cross-sectional study comparing gray matter volumes in post-stroke patients with and without depression using voxel-based morphometry.
dataset_features_summary: High-resolution T1-weighted MRI scans from 56 chronic subcortical stroke patients
Voxel-based morphometry (VBM) analysis of gray matter volume
Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HAMD) scores for depressive symptom severity
Clinical and demographic variables including age, sex, education, illness duration, and total intracranial volume
website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study analyzed high-resolution T1-weighted MRI scans from 56 chronic subcortical stroke patients to compare gray matter volumes between those with post-stroke depression (PSD) and non-PSD controls. The results showed significantly decreased gray matter volume in the left middle frontal gyrus among PSD patients, which correlated with depressive symptom severity measured by the Hamilton Depression Rating Scale. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI data, HAMD scores, and demographic/clinical variables for a matched cohort of chronic stroke survivors.

website_card.short_description: A prospective longitudinal study using baseline cortical thickness and machine learning to predict the first onset of major depression in never-disordered adolescent girls.
dataset_features_summary: T1-weighted structural MRI scans from a cohort of never-disordered adolescents aged 10-15 years
Longitudinal clinical follow-up over up to 5 years with reassessments every 18 months for depressive disorder onset

website_card.plain_text_summary: This study followed a cohort of never-disordered adolescent girls aged 10 to 15 years over five years to predict the first onset of major depressive disorder. Baseline T1-weighted MRI scans were processed to extract regional cortical thickness, which was then used with support vector machine classification to distinguish future cases from those who remained healthy. Replication would require access to baseline structural MRI data, longitudinal clinical diagnostic assessments for depression and anxiety, and standardized symptom rating scales collected over multiple timepoints.

title: Brain hyperintensity location determines outcome in the triad of impaired cognition, physical health and depressive symptoms: A cohort study in late life
citation: Murray, A., McNeil, C., Salarirad, S., Deary, I., Phillips, L., Whalley, L., & Staff, R. (2016). Brain hyperintensity location determines outcome in the triad of impaired cognition, physical health and depressive symptoms: A cohort study in late life. Archives of Gerontology and Geriatrics, 63, 49–54. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.archger.2015.10.004

website_card.short_description: A cohort study examining how the location of MRI-detected brain hyperintensities relates to depressive symptoms, cognition, and physical health in older adults.
dataset_features_summary: T1-weighted MRI data used for visual scoring of white matter, grey matter, and infratentorial hyperintensities via Scheltens scale
Cognitive assessment scores including premorbid IQ (NART), memory (AVLT), executive function (UCO), processing speed (Digit Symbol), and fluid intelligence (Raven's PM)
Depressive symptom severity measured using the Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS-D)
Physical health measures comprising walk time and peak expiratory flow rate (PEFR)
website_card.plain_text_summary: This study analyzed T1-weighted MRI scans, cognitive test scores, physical fitness measures, and depressive symptom ratings from approximately 205 older adults in the Aberdeen 1936 Birth Cohort. The main finding was that hyperintensities located in deep brain structures (grey matter and infratentorial regions) were associated with increased depressive symptoms, a relationship largely mediated by poorer physical health and cognitive ability rather than direct effects. Replication would require access to scored MRI lesion location data, standardized cognitive test results, HADS depression scores, and objective physical performance metrics.

extraction.notes: Extracted features based on explicit mentions of T1w MRI acquisition, visual hyperintensity scoring (Scheltens scale), cognitive battery, HADS depression scores, and physical health measures. VBM/voxel-wise analysis was explicitly not performed. Study design is cross-sectional despite originating from a longitudinal birth cohort. Age group assigned as older_adults based on mean age ~68 years.

title: Dispositional mindfulness co-varies with smaller amygdala and caudate volumes in community adults
citation: Taren, A. A., Creswell, J. D., & Gianaros, P. J. (2013). Dispositional mindfulness co-varies with smaller amygdala and caudate volumes in community adults. PLoS ONE, 8(5). https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0064574

website_card.short_description: A cross-sectional study examining the association between dispositional mindfulness and subcortical brain volumes in healthy community adults.
dataset_features_summary: T1-weighted MRI scans from 155 healthy community adults
Dispositional mindfulness scores measured via the MAAS questionnaire
Depression severity (BDI) and anxiety severity (STAI) scale scores
Subcortical volumetric data for amygdala, caudate, hippocampus, and nucleus accumbens
website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study analyzed T1-weighted MRI scans from 155 healthy community adults to examine how dispositional mindfulness relates to subcortical brain volumes. Higher dispositional mindfulness was associated with smaller grey matter volumes in the right amygdala and left caudate, even after controlling for age, total grey matter volume, depression severity, and neuroticism. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI data, MAAS mindfulness scores, BDI depression scores, STAI anxiety scores, and demographic information including age and gender.

title: Genetic patterning for child psychopathology is distinct from that for adults and implicates fetal cerebellar development
citation: Hughes, D. E., Kunitoki, K., Elyounssi, S., Luo, M., Bazer, O. M., Hopkinson, C. E., Dowling, K. F., Doyle, A. E., Dunn, E. C., Eryilmaz, H., Gilman, J. M., Holt, D. J., Valera, E. website_card.short_description: A genetic association study examining how polygenic risk for neurodevelopmental traits relates to childhood psychopathology and brain volume measures in the ABCD and Generation R cohorts.
dataset_features_summary: Polygenic scores (PGS) derived from GWAS summary statistics
Childhood psychopathology symptom scales (CBCL, PQ-BC)
Brain volume metrics including cerebellar lobule I-V and cortical volumes
Longitudinal data at baseline/year 2 (ABCD) and ages 9/13 (Generation R)
website_card.plain_text_summary: This study analyzes polygenic risk scores for neurodevelopmental traits in relation to childhood psychopathology symptoms and brain volume measures using data from the ABCD and Generation R cohorts. The research utilizes longitudinal symptom assessments across multiple age points alongside genetic data to identify distinct patterning of genetic risk in children versus adults. Replication would require access to GWAS-derived polygenic scores, standardized behavioral rating scales (e.g., CBCL), and structural brain volume metrics for child/adolescent populations.

title: Depressive symptoms in cognitively unimpaired older adults are associated with lower structural and functional integrity in a frontolimbic network
citation: Touron, E., Moulinet, I., Kuhn, E., Sherif, S., Ourry, V., Landeau, B., Mézenge, F., Vivien, D., Klimecki, O. M., Poisnel, G., Marchant, N. L., & Chételat, G. (2022). website_card.short_description: A cross-sectional study examining the association between subclinical depressive symptoms and brain structural and functional integrity in cognitively unimpaired older adults using multimodal neuroimaging.
dataset_features_summary: T1-weighted MRI scans for gray matter volume analysis
FDG-PET and amyloid-PET imaging data
Geriatric Depression Scale (GDS) scores
State-Trait Anxiety Inventory (STAI-B) scores
Cognitive test scores including MMSE, MoCA, DRS, and verbal learning tests
APOE ε4 carrier status
website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study analyzed T1-weighted MRI and PET imaging data from cognitively unimpaired older adults in the Age-Well and ADNI cohorts to assess brain structural and functional changes associated with subclinical depressive symptoms. Participants with higher depressive symptom scores showed lower gray matter volume in the hippocampus, reduced white matter integrity, and decreased glucose metabolism in frontolimbic regions, but no association with amyloid deposition. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI scans, FDG-PET and amyloid-PET imaging data, GDS depression scores, anxiety scale scores, cognitive assessment results, and APOE genotype information from community-dwelling older adults aged 65 and above.

extraction.notes: Extracted features based on explicit mentions of T1 MRI, voxel-wise GM volume analysis, GDS and STAI-B scales, APOE status, cognitive tests, and exclusion criteria for medication. Longitudinal data explicitly ruled out by authors. MDD patients excluded; healthy/low-symptom controls used.

extraction_notes: Extracted features based on explicit mentions of T1 MRI, voxel-wise GM volume analysis, GDS and STAI-B scales, APOE status, cognitive tests, and exclusion criteria for medication. citation: Seminowicz, D. A., Shpaner, M., Keaser, M. L., Krauthamer, G. M., Mantegna, J., Dumas, J. A., Newhouse, P. A., Filippi, C. G., Keefe, F. J., & Naylor, M. R. (2013). website_card.short_description: This study investigates gray matter changes in patients with chronic pain following an 11-week cognitive-behavioral therapy intervention using voxel-based morphometry of T1-weighted MRI scans.
dataset_features_summary: T1-weighted MRI scans acquired pre- and post-intervention for patients, plus baseline scans for healthy controls.
Voxel-based morphometry (VBM) analysis of structural brain data.
Clinical assessments including Beck Depression Inventory (BDI), pain catastrophizing scales, and chronic pain duration.
website_card.plain_text_summary: The study examined 13 patients with mixed chronic pain conditions and 13 healthy controls using T1-weighted MRI scans before and after an 11-week cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) intervention. Patients showed increased gray matter volume in prefrontal and somatosensory regions following CBT, which correlated with reduced pain catastrophizing. Replication would require access to pre- and post-intervention T1-weighted MRI scans from chronic pain patients, healthy control data, and standardized clinical measures including depression scores and pain coping assessments.

extraction.notes: Extracted features based on explicit mentions of T1-weighted MRI, VBM analysis, chronic pain patients vs healthy controls, BDI depression scale, pre-post intervention design, and illness duration. No genetic, anxiety, medication, trauma, cognitive battery, or blood biomarker data were reported.

MADRS depression severity scores
Levodopa medication dosage data
Cognitive assessment scores (MMSE, WAIS)
Demographic data including age and sex
website_card.plain_text_summary: This study used T1-weighted MRI scans from 50 Parkinson's disease patients (23 depressed, 27 nondepressed) and 16 healthy controls to examine gray matter density differences. Depressed PD patients showed reduced gray matter in the orbitofrontal cortex and temporal regions compared to nondepressed PD patients, with severity correlating with MADRS scores. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI data, MADRS depression ratings, levodopa dosage records, and cognitive assessment scores for matched clinical and control groups.

title: Depression is associated with hippocampal volume loss in adults with HIV
citation: Bronshteyn, M., Yang, F. N., Shattuck, K. F., Dawson, M., Kumar, P., Moore, D. J., Ellis, R. J., & Jiang, X. (2021). Depression is associated with hippocampal volume loss in adults with HIV. Human Brain Mapping, 42(12), 3750–3759. https://doi.org/10.1002/hbm.25451

website_card.short_description: A cross-sectional MRI study examining the association between depressive symptoms and hippocampal gray matter volume in adults with chronic HIV infection.
dataset_features_summary: T1-weighted structural MRI scans from 100 persons with HIV
Beck Depression Inventory-II (BDI-II) scores for depression severity
Comprehensive neuropsychological test battery results across seven domains
Blood biomarkers including CD4 counts, CD8 counts, and HIV viral load
Self-reported antidepressant medication status and history
website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study analyzed T1-weighted MRI scans from 100 adults with chronic HIV to investigate the relationship between depressive symptoms and hippocampal gray matter volume. Participants with higher depression scores showed reduced hippocampal volume, particularly in the right hemisphere, independent of antidepressant use or current HIV disease severity markers like CD4 nadir. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI data, BDI-II depression scores, neuropsychological test results, and clinical blood biomarkers (CD4 counts, viral load) from a similar adult population with chronic HIV infection.

extraction.notes: Extracted features based on explicit mentions of T1-weighted MRI, VBM analysis, BDI-II depression scores, neuropsychological testing, and blood biomarkers (CD4/viral load). No healthy controls or longitudinal data were used. MDD diagnosis was explicitly noted as unavailable in the limitations.

website_card.short_description: A large-scale structural MRI study examining how cortical thickness and structural covariance networks correlate with clinical response to rTMS in patients with major depressive disorder compared to healthy controls.
dataset_features_summary: Pre- and post-treatment T1-weighted high-resolution MRI scans
Vertex-based cortical thickness, volume, and surface area maps derived via Freesurfer
Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HRSD-17) scores for clinical response assessment
Medication status records (antidepressants and benzodiazepines)
Healthy control group demographic and structural MRI data
website_card.plain_text_summary: This study analyzed pre- and post-treatment T1-weighted MRI scans from 268 adults with major depressive disorder and 70 healthy controls to investigate structural brain network changes following repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS). Clinical improvement, measured by the HRSD-17 depression scale, was associated with post-treatment changes in cortical thickness networks centered on the stimulated DLPFC region. Replication would require access to pre- and post-intervention T1-weighted MRI scans, standardized depression rating scores, and detailed medication history for both clinical and control participants.

extraction.notes: Extracted features based on explicit mentions of T1 MRI, MDD/HC groups, HRSD-17 scale, longitudinal imaging, and medication status. dataset_features_summary: T1-weighted MRI scans used for tissue segmentation
31P Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopy (MRS) data measuring phosphocreatine, inorganic phosphate, and NTP metabolites
HAM-D-17 and Symptom Questionnaire scores for depression severity
Demographic data including age and sex
website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study compared brain bioenergetic metabolite levels using 31P MRS in unmedicated adults with major depressive disorder (MDD) versus healthy controls. Researchers found tissue-specific differences in phosphocreatine and inorganic phosphate between gray and white matter, which correlated with depression severity scores. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI scans for tissue segmentation, 31P MRS acquisition parameters, and standardized depression rating scale data from both clinical and control groups.

dataset_features_summary: Baseline T1-weighted MRI scans acquired pre-ECT
Visual rating scales for white matter hyperintensities, medial temporal lobe atrophy, and global cortical atrophy
Mini Mental State Examination (MMSE) scores measured at baseline, during ECT, and up to 6 months post-treatment
Montgomery-Åsberg Depression Rating Scale (MADRS) scores for depression severity
Demographic and clinical data including age of onset, education level, and medication status
website_card.plain_text_summary: This study evaluated cognitive trajectories in 80 older patients with severe late-life depression undergoing electroconvulsive therapy (ECT), using baseline T1-weighted MRI scans to assess structural brain characteristics like white matter hyperintensities. Cognitive function was measured repeatedly before, during, and up to six months after ECT using the MMSE, alongside depression severity ratings via the MADRS. Replication would require access to pre-treatment structural MRI data, repeated cognitive assessment scores across multiple timepoints, and clinical records detailing medication status and depression history.

Volumetric measurements of 24 predefined brain regions using atlas-based parcellation (ALP technique).
Clinical data including Hamilton Depression Rating Scale scores, illness duration, age of onset, medication status, and cognitive assessments.
website_card.plain_text_summary: This study examined gray matter volumes in elderly patients with late-life depression compared to non-depressed controls using high-resolution T1-weighted MRI. Participants were 70 years or older, and brain region volumes were measured using an atlas-based parcellation technique rather than voxel-wise morphometry. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI scans, structured clinical interview data for depression diagnosis, Hamilton Depression Rating Scale scores, illness duration/onset dates, medication history, and cognitive assessment results.

website_card.plain_text_summary: This study analyzed preoperative T1-weighted MRI scans from 27 patients with treatment-resistant depression to identify structural brain predictors of response to subcallosal cingulate deep brain stimulation. Responders showed significantly larger volumes in the subcallosal cingulate gyrus, thalamus, and amygdala, along with smaller cortical grey matter volume compared to nonresponders. Replication would require access to preoperative structural MRI scans, 12-month clinical depression severity scores (HRSD-17), and detailed demographic/medication history for the patient cohort.

website_card.plain_text_summary: This study analyzed T1-weighted structural and functional MRI scans from 25 healthy adults to examine how brain structure, function, and connectivity relate to the cortisol awakening response. Participants completed a psychosocial stress task in the scanner while salivary cortisol was measured across multiple time points, revealing that CAR magnitude correlated with grey matter volume and stress-related activity in the perigenual anterior cingulate cortex. Replication would require access to raw structural and functional MRI data, detailed salivary cortisol sampling protocols, and standardized depression screening scores to verify participant health status.

website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study analyzed T1-weighted MRI data from 74 patients with mild Alzheimer's disease or Lewy body dementia to examine cortical thickness in relation to depressive symptoms and antidepressant use. Depressed patients showed significant cortical thinning in prefrontal and temporal regions, while antidepressant use was associated with thinning in the parahippocampal region. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI scans, MADRS depression scores, medication history, and clinical dementia diagnoses for elderly participants.

website_card.short_description: This study investigates structural and functional brain abnormalities in Itpr2 knockout mice exhibiting depressive-like behavior, linking them to decreased striatal BDNF expression.
dataset_features_summary: Structural T2-weighted MRI and resting-state fMRI data from Itpr2 knockout mice and healthy controls.
Voxel-based morphometry and seed-based functional connectivity analyses of the striatum and related brain regions.
Behavioral test scores including sucrose preference, tail suspension, forced swim, and open-field tests.
BDNF protein expression levels measured via Western blotting and immunofluorescence in striatal tissue and astrocytes.
website_card.plain_text_summary: This animal study used structural MRI and resting-state fMRI to compare brain structure and function between Itpr2 knockout mice with depressive-like behaviors and healthy controls. The researchers found reduced gray matter volume in the striatum and altered functional connectivity, which correlated with decreased mature BDNF expression in striatal tissue and astrocytes. Replication would require access to raw MRI scans from genetically modified mouse models, standardized behavioral test data, and protein quantification assays for neurotrophin analysis.

Task-related fMRI during visuo-spatial n-back working memory paradigm

website_card.plain_text_summary: The study used T1-weighted MRI, task-based fMRI, and diffusion tensor imaging in 48 temporal lobe epilepsy patients and 30 healthy volunteers to examine default mode network deactivation during a working memory task. Participants with a lifetime history of affective disorders showed significantly greater deactivation in the subgenual anterior cingulate cortex compared to both TLE-only patients and healthy controls, independent of current mood or medication status. Replication would require access to raw structural and functional MRI data, standardized depression and anxiety scale scores, and detailed clinical psychiatric diagnoses for each participant.

website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study analyzed baseline T1-weighted MRI scans from 41 depressed older adults to compare cortical thickness and hippocampal volume between patients with and without vascular disease. Replication would require access to raw longitudinal MADRS scores, baseline and follow-up blood sample data for metabolic/biomarker analysis, and detailed documentation of medication exclusion criteria and tsDCS stimulation parameters.

website_card.short_description: A case-control study examining the association between striatal gray matter volume, delay discounting behavior, and suicide attempts in older adults with late-life depression.

website_card.plain_text_summary: This study compared high-resolution T1-weighted MRI scans, clinical diagnoses, and behavioral tasks in 52 older adults (aged 60+) with late-life depression, including suicide attempters, non-suicidal depressed patients, and healthy controls. Suicide attempters showed reduced gray matter voxel counts in the putamen and associative striatum, which correlated with higher delay discounting of delayed rewards. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI data, detailed clinical interview records for depression and suicide history, standardized cognitive and behavioral task scores, and medication treatment histories.

extraction.notes: Extracted features based on explicit mentions of T1 MRI, clinical groups (MDD with/without suicide attempts vs healthy controls), age range (~60+ years), and assessment tools (HAMD-17, MCQ, CGT, DRS, MMSE). Longitudinal data was collected but not used in this analysis. Atlas-based ROI segmentation was used rather than whole-brain VBM.

Depression severity scores (HAMD, SDS)
Anxiety severity scores (HAMA, SAS)
Insomnia severity scores (PSQI, HAMD-S)
Medication status/history
website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study used structural T1-weighted MRI to compare cortical thickness and volume in 65 MDD patients and 67 primary insomnia patients. The main finding was that the interaction of co-occurring depression and insomnia was associated with decreased gray matter volume in the right orbitofrontal cortex. Replication would require access to raw structural MRI scans, clinical diagnoses for MDD and PI, standardized depression/anxiety/insomnia rating scales, and medication history data.

citation: Li, M., Yan, Y., Jia, H., Gao, Y., Qiu, J., & Yang, W. (2024). Neural basis underlying the association between thought control ability and happiness: The moderating role of the amygdala. PsyCh Journal, 13(4), 625–638. https://doi.org/10.1002/pchj.741

website_card.short_description: A cross-sectional VBM study investigating the neural basis of thought control ability and happiness in healthy young adults, highlighting the amygdala's moderating role.
dataset_features_summary: T1-weighted structural MRI scans from 314 healthy college students
Voxel-based morphometry (VBM) analysis for gray matter density
Self-report questionnaires: Thought Control Ability Questionnaire (TCAQ), Oxford Happiness Questionnaire (OHQ)
Clinical covariates: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI-II) and Beck Anxiety Inventory (BAI) scores
website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study examined the relationship between thought control ability, happiness, and brain structure in 314 healthy young adult college students. Participants completed self-report measures of thought control and happiness while undergoing T1-weighted MRI scanning. Voxel-based morphometry revealed that gray matter density in the bilateral amygdala negatively correlated with thought control ability and moderated its association with happiness. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI data, standardized questionnaires for thought control and happiness (e.g., TCAQ, OHQ), and demographic/clinical covariate scores (age, sex, BDI-II, BAI).

Resting-state functional connectivity (rsFC) data acquired across multiple sites using a parcellation atlas.
Comprehensive cognitive assessment battery covering eight domains including working memory, processing speed, and verbal learning.
Clinical symptom scales (PANSS, BDI) and functioning measures (GAF).
Medication history quantified via chlorpromazine equivalents.
website_card.plain_text_summary: This study analyzed structural MRI and resting-state functional connectivity data from patients with recent-onset psychosis, recent-onset depression, clinical high-risk for psychosis, and healthy controls to identify transdiagnostic cognitive subgroups. Participants underwent comprehensive neuropsychological testing and clinical symptom assessments at a single timepoint. Replication would require access to raw sMRI and rsfMRI scans, detailed cognitive test scores across multiple domains, and standardized clinical rating scales (PANSS, BDI) alongside medication history data.

title: Basal ganglia morphology links the metabolic syndrome and depressive symptoms
citation: Onyewuenyi, I. C., Muldoon, M. F., Christie, I. C., Erickson, K. I., & Gianaros, P. J. (2014). Basal ganglia morphology links the metabolic syndrome and depressive symptoms. Physiology & Behavior, 123, 214–222. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.physbeh.2013.09.014

website_card.short_description: Cross-sectional study examining the association between metabolic syndrome, basal ganglia volumes, and depressive symptoms in healthy middle-aged adults.
dataset_features_summary: T1-weighted MRI scans for automated ROI segmentation of basal ganglia regions (caudate, putamen, nucleus accumbens, globus pallidus).
Metabolic syndrome criteria data including fasting lipids, glucose, waist circumference, and blood pressure.
Depressive symptom severity scores measured via the PHQ-9 questionnaire.
Demographic and health covariates: age, sex, race, education, smoking status, total brain volume, and medication history.
website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study analyzed T1-weighted MRI scans from 147 healthy middle-aged adults to examine how metabolic syndrome relates to basal ganglia morphology and depressive symptoms. The results showed that meeting more metabolic syndrome criteria was associated with reduced globus pallidus volume, which statistically mediated the link between metabolic syndrome and higher depressive symptom scores. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI data, fasting blood biomarkers for metabolic syndrome assessment, PHQ-9 depression scores, and demographic/health covariates.

extraction.notes: Extracted features based on explicit mentions in the text. T1-weighted MRI was acquired and used for ROI-based segmentation (FIRST), not VBM/voxel-wise. The sample consisted entirely of healthy adults with no MDD patients. Depressive symptoms were measured via PHQ-9, metabolic syndrome components required fasting blood draws, medication status was recorded as an exclusion/covariate factor, and Verbal IQ was reported in Table 1. Main analysis is cross-sectional.

website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study compared 21 Alzheimer’s disease patients with depression symptoms to 9 AD patients without depression and 21 healthy controls using T1-weighted MRI and arterial spin labeling (ASL) perfusion imaging. Replication would require access to raw ASL and T1w MRI data, HAMD scores, cognitive test results (MMSE/MoCA), and precise demographic information for AD patients stratified by depression status alongside healthy controls.

website_card.short_description: A randomized controlled pilot study investigating the effects of long-term airborne infrasound exposure on brain structure, cognition, and mental health in healthy adults.
dataset_features_summary: T1-weighted structural MRI scans acquired pre- and post-intervention
Voxel-based morphometry (VBM) analysis of regional grey matter volume changes
Standardized psychiatric symptom assessments including depression and anxiety subscales (BSI)
Computerized cognitive performance testing covering alertness, attention, and working memory
Sleep quality and fatigue questionnaires (PSQI, ESS)
Socio-demographic data and regular medication status records
website_card.plain_text_summary: This study examined the effects of one month of continuous airborne infrasound exposure on brain structure, cognition, and mental health in 38 healthy young adults. Using pre- and post-intervention T1-weighted MRI scans analyzed with voxel-based morphometry, researchers found no global increases but observed localized decreases in regional grey matter volume within the cerebellum and angular gyrus in the infrasound group compared to placebo. Replication would require access to raw structural MRI data, standardized psychiatric and cognitive assessment scores, and detailed exposure parameters for controlled infrasound environments.

website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study examined T1-weighted MRI scans and retrospective childhood adversity reports in a community-dwelling cohort of 398 older adults aged 65 to 80. The main results indicate that early-life adverse events are associated with widespread grey matter volume alterations, moderated by sex and 5-HTTLPR genotype. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI data, childhood trauma questionnaires, 5-HTTLPR genotyping results, and demographic/clinical covariates.

Longitudinal data was not used for the main analysis as the study explicitly employed a cross-sectional design.

website_card.plain_text_summary: This study analyzed 136 inpatients with bipolar disorder to determine how the 5-HTTLPR genotype moderates the relationship between early life stress and adult suicidality, age at illness onset, and regional gray matter volumes. A subsample of 116 participants underwent T1-weighted MRI scanning, while all participants provided blood for genotyping and completed retrospective stress assessments using the Social Readjustment Rating Scale. Replication would require access to raw structural MRI scans, 5-HTTLPR genotype data, detailed early life stress histories, and clinical records tracking suicide attempts and illness onset in bipolar patients.

website_card.plain_text_summary: This study used T1-weighted MRI scans from 14 inpatients with major depressive disorder, assessed at three timepoints during a course of electroconvulsive therapy. The researchers found that gray matter volume increases in the hippocampus were associated with late antidepressant effects, while volume changes in the temporal gyrus correlated with early antipsychotic symptom improvement. Replication would require access to longitudinal structural MRI data, HDRS and MMSE clinical scores, and detailed medication records for MDD patients undergoing ECT.

Researchers found that greater exposure to adverse life events and recent stress was associated with smaller gray matter volume in medial prefrontal, anterior cingulate, and insula regions. Replication would require access to raw structural MRI data, detailed lifetime trauma/stress interview scores, depression severity ratings, and demographic covariates like age, sex, and education level.

website_card.short_description: This cross-sectional study investigates the associations between menopause-related factors, lifetime broad depression, and APOE ε4 genotype with proxies of cellular aging (leukocyte telomere length) and brain aging (brain age gap) in UK Biobank females.
dataset_features_summary: T1-weighted MRI data
APOE ε4 genotype data
Leukocyte telomere length (LTL) from blood samples
Lifetime broad depression status based on self-report and ICD codes
Menopausal status and reproductive history
website_card.plain_text_summary: Data used / population / imaging modality: T1-weighted MRI and diffusion MRI from 13,780 females in the UK Biobank cohort (ages 39–82), along with leukocyte telomere length measurements and APOE ε4 genotype data. Main broad result: Postmenopausal status and older age at natural menopause were associated with longer leukocyte telomere length and lower brain age gap, while lifetime broad depression and APOE ε4 genotype consistently explained the greatest variance in these aging proxies. Key data requirements useful for replication: Replication would require access to UK Biobank T1-weighted MRI scans, diffusion MRI data, leukocyte telomere length measurements, APOE ε4 genotype data, and detailed self-reported or clinical records of menopausal history and lifetime depression status.

website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study analyzed structural T1-weighted MRI scans from patients with behavioral and aphasic variants of frontotemporal dementia to investigate the neuroanatomical correlates of apathy and depression. website_card.plain_text_summary: The study analyzed T1-weighted MRI, diffusion tensor imaging, and resting-state fMRI data from 36 patients with lumbar disc herniation and 38 age- and gender-matched healthy controls. Results indicated that chronic pain intensity was significantly predicted by depression scores, which were mediated by increased temporal coupling between the thalamus and dorsolateral prefrontal cortex in the patient group. Replication would require access to raw structural and functional MRI scans, diffusion data, and standardized psychological and pain assessment scales for both clinical and control cohorts.

title: Baseline clinical and neuroimaging biomarkers of treatment response to high-frequency rTMS over the left DLPFC for resistant depression
citation: Harika-Germaneau, G., Wassouf, I., le Tutour, T., Guillevin, R., Doolub, D., Rostami, R., Delbreil, A., Langbour, N., & Jaafari, N. (2022). Baseline clinical and neuroimaging biomarkers of treatment response to high-frequency rTMS over the left DLPFC for resistant depression. Frontiers in Psychiatry, 13. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyt.2022.894473

website_card.short_description: This study investigates clinical and structural neuroimaging biomarkers associated with treatment response to high-frequency rTMS over the left DLPFC in patients with treatment-resistant depression.
dataset_features_summary: Baseline T1-weighted MRI scans processed with FreeSurfer for cortical thickness and volume measures
Longitudinal clinical assessments using MADRS and BDI scales at baseline, post-treatment, and 1-month follow-up
Detailed medication history and status data
Demographic information including age and gender
Clinical diagnosis classification (Major Depressive Disorder vs. Bipolar Depression)
website_card.plain_text_summary: The study analyzed baseline T1-weighted MRI scans and clinical assessments from 95 patients with treatment-resistant depression undergoing high-frequency rTMS over the left DLPFC. Clinical response was defined by a 50% reduction in MADRS scores, with responders showing lower gray matter volume in specific frontal regions compared to non-responders. Replication would require access to baseline structural MRI data, longitudinal clinical rating scales (MADRS/BDI), and detailed medication history for patients with treatment-resistant depression.

extraction.notes: Extracted features based on explicit mentions of T1 MRI, MADRS/BDI scales, medication status, and clinical diagnoses. Longitudinal data refers to clinical follow-up assessments rather than repeated imaging. No healthy controls or genetic/blood biomarker data were reported.

website_card.short_description: A longitudinal MRI study tracking white matter hyperintensity accumulation and structural brain changes during a 12-week antidepressant treatment course in elderly patients with late-life depression.
dataset_features_summary: Paired baseline and 12-week follow-up T1-weighted MRI scans for gray matter and hippocampal volume
FLAIR/T2-weighted MRI sequences for white matter hyperintensity quantification
Diffusion tensor imaging data for fractional anisotropy assessment

Mini-Mental State Exam (MMSE) cognitive screening results
Antidepressant medication type and final dosage records
website_card.plain_text_summary: This study evaluated 47 elderly patients with late-life depression who underwent baseline and 12-week follow-up MRI scans while receiving antidepressant treatment. Researchers measured changes in white matter hyperintensities, gray matter volume, hippocampal volume, and fractional anisotropy, correlating these imaging markers with remission status determined by standardized depression rating scales (HRSD/MADRS). Replication would require access to paired structural MRI datasets, detailed medication dosing logs, and clinical depression severity scores for older adult patients.

website_card.plain_text_summary: This study analyzed T1-weighted MRI and diffusion tensor imaging data from 36 older adults with late-life depression to investigate how age at onset correlates with brain structure. Later age at onset was linked to reduced white matter integrity in frontal tracts, while earlier onset correlated with smaller hippocampal volumes. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI and DTI scans, detailed psychiatric history including age of first depressive episode, and standardized depression rating scales.

Psychological Medicine, 54(1), 108–116. https://doi.org/10.1017/S0033291722003555

website_card.short_description: A six-month longitudinal study examining the independent relationships between structural brain volume changes, peripheral inflammation markers, and depressive symptom trajectories in patients following an index electroconvulsive therapy course.
dataset_features_summary: Paired structural MRI scans acquired at end of ECT and 6-month follow-up
Peripheral blood biomarkers (IL-6, IL-8, TNFα, CRP) measured at both timepoints
HAM-D depression severity scores collected longitudinally

website_card.plain_text_summary: This study followed 20 patients with treatment-resistant depression for six months after an index course of electroconvulsive therapy (ECT). Researchers collected structural MRI scans and peripheral blood samples at the end of treatment and again at follow-up to assess changes in gray matter volume and inflammatory markers. The results showed that decreased gray matter volume in specific brain regions and reduced levels of the inflammatory marker IL-8 were independently associated with improvements in depressive symptoms over time. Replication would require access to paired structural MRI scans, peripheral blood biomarker data, and longitudinal depression severity scores from patients undergoing ECT.
