title: Glial fibrillary acidic protein in late life major depressive disorder: An immunocytochemical study
citation: Davis, S., Thomas, A., Perry, R., Oakley, A., Kalaria, R. N., & O’Brien, J. T. (2002). Glial fibrillary acidic protein in late life major depressive disorder: An immunocytochemical study. Journal of Neurology, Neurosurgery & Psychiatry, 73(5), 556–560. https://doi.org/10.1136/jnnp.73.5.556

website_card.short_description: Postmortem immunocytochemistry study measuring GFAP levels in the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex and anterior cingulate cortex of elderly MDD patients versus controls.
dataset_features_summary: Postmortem brain tissue from 20 elderly MDD patients and 20 age-matched controls
Immunocytochemical staining for GFAP across cortical layers in DLPFC and ACC
Quantitative image analysis of GFAP areal fraction
website_card.plain_text_summary: This study analyzed postmortem brain tissue from 20 elderly patients with major depressive disorder and 20 age-matched healthy controls to measure glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP) immunoreactivity in the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex and anterior cingulate cortex. The researchers found significantly higher GFAP levels only in layer I of the DLPFC in depressed subjects, providing limited support for the vascular depression hypothesis. Replication would require access to postmortem brain tissue samples from elderly MDD patients and controls, along with standardized immunocytochemistry protocols for GFAP quantification across specific cortical layers.

extraction.notes: Extracted features based on explicit mentions of postmortem tissue, MDD patients, controls, and GFAP immunocytochemistry. Clinical scales, genetic data, and blood biomarkers were not reported.

website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study compared T1-weighted MRI scans from 59 young women (mean age ~23) with a family history of depression, divided into a resilient high-risk group and a major depressive disorder group. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI data, HAM-D and STAI clinical scores, childhood trauma questionnaires, and detailed medication history for the depressed cohort.

website_card.short_description: A longitudinal investigation examining how lifetime social threat severity relates to fronto-cingulate-limbic gray matter morphometry and depressive symptom trajectories in depressed adolescents over nine months.
dataset_features_summary: Structural MRI data from 57 clinically diagnosed depressed adolescents (mean age ~16 years)

website_card.plain_text_summary: This longitudinal study analyzed structural MRI scans from 57 depressed adolescents to examine how lifetime social threat stressors relate to gray matter morphometry in fronto-cingulate-limbic regions and depressive symptoms over a 9-month period. Greater social threat severity was associated with smaller amygdala and nucleus accumbens volumes, as well as reduced anterior cingulate cortex surface area, which mediated the link between stress and worsening depression. Replication would require access to structural MRI scans, lifetime stressor inventory data (STRAIN), repeated depression/anxiety scale scores over 9 months, and medication status records for adolescent clinical samples.

Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HAMD) scores for depression severity assessment
Demographic and clinical history records including age, sex, education, medication status, and age of onset
website_card.plain_text_summary: This cross-sectional study compared T1-weighted structural MRI and resting-state functional MRI scans from 13 older adults with late-life depression and 24 age- and sex-matched healthy controls. The analysis revealed significant gray matter volume alterations and increased local brain activity in specific regions among the depressed group, which positively correlated with HAMD scores. Replication would require access to raw T1-weighted MRI and rs-fMRI data, Hamilton Depression Rating Scale assessments, and detailed demographic/clinical history records.
